columns.concentrationTooltip: Vendor Concentration: 1.0 = single vendor, 0.0 = perfectly competitive

heatmap.noCritical: No institutions with critical risk in the current sample.
heatmap.headline: The Institutional Corruption Thermometer
heatmap.subtitle: Each cell represents the average risk score of an institution. Red does not lie.
heatmap.lede: Of the {{n}} institutions mapped, those with the highest vendor concentration and direct-award rates also register the highest risk scores. This map reveals where purchasing power accumulates — and where oversight is most urgent.
heatmap.fullMapQuote: “Money leaves a trail. These diagrams show where every peso goes and who dominates each institution.”
heatmap.sankeyDesc: Left nodes = institutions. Right nodes = vendors. Link width = contract value. Color = risk level. Click a node to view the profile.
heatmap.hhiDesc: HHI (Herfindahl-Hirschman Index) measures vendor concentration. HHI>0.25 indicates a single vendor dominates. Click a bar to view the institution.
heatmap.zonasRojasDesc: Ranked by average risk score. Click to view the full institution profile.
heatmap.top5Desc: Measured by the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI). HHI > 0.25 indicates a single vendor dominates that institution's spending.
heatmap.methodologyText: Risk scores are computed using RUBLI model v6.5: a calibrated logistic regression with 9 statistical indicators (price volatility, vendor concentration, direct awards, among others), normalized by sector and year. A score is not proof of corruption — it measures similarity to documented patterns of irregularities in Mexican public procurement. Concentration is measured with the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI): a value above 0.25 indicates a single vendor dominates that institution's purchasing.

publicationDelay.title: Publication Delay Transparency
publicationDelay.description: Days between procedure publication and contract award. Shorter windows compress vendor preparation time and favour pre-selected suppliers.

health.headline: Institutions Under Vendor Control
health.subtitle: An analysis of the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index reveals which agencies have been captured by a single vendor
health.lede: When a single company wins more than 50% of an agency's contracts, experts call it institutional capture. In Mexico, {{count}} agencies exhibit this pattern, concentrating {{amount}} in public spending under the control of a handful of vendors.
health.top3Subtitle: The agencies where a single vendor has the most absolute control over public spending.
health.rankingsSubtitle: Click headers to sort. Red-bordered rows indicate capture (HHI â‰¥ 0.50); orange border indicates high concentration.
health.impactText: Institutional capture in healthcare means essential medicines are procured from a single supplier, eliminating competition and raising prices. In infrastructure, it means public works are awarded without real competition. In every case, the citizen pays more for less.
health.loadError: Could not load data. Try reloading the page.

methodology.label: Methodology
methodology.question: What is the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index and why does it matter?
methodology.pullQuote: An HHI of 1.0 means all of an agency's money goes to a single supplier. It is the statistical definition of institutional capture.
methodology.body1: The Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) measures how concentrated spending is within an institution. It is calculated by summing the squares of each vendor's market share. An HHI near 0 indicates high competition; an HHI of 1.0 indicates absolute monopoly.
methodology.body2: In public contracts, extreme concentration is a red flag: it suggests a vendor has eliminated competition, whether through legitimate capacity, political connections, or collusion with officials.
methodology.noteText: Note: HHI here is reported on a 0â€“1 scale (sum of squares of decimal market shares), where 1.0 represents absolute monopoly. The traditional 0â€“10,000 scale is divided by 10,000 for ease of comparison.

vendors.efosNote: Cross-reference with EFOS (SAT Art. 69-B) and SFP sanctions available on vendor profiles. Click any vendor to verify external registry status.

officials.methodologyNote: Based on Coviello & Gagliarducci (2017) â€” official tenure correlates with single-bidder rates. Data available for 2018+ contracts (COMPRANET Structure C/D).

external.noAsfData: No ASF audit findings on record
